chosen_title: Selling inherited property or ancestral gold — how it's actually taxed — Mojo explainer
caption:
Your grandmother left you her house. There's no inheritance tax in India — but the capital gains bill when you sell? That's a different story.

Under Section 49 of the Income Tax Act, the original owner's purchase price and date of acquisition carry forward to you. Every rupee of price rise since your ancestor first bought the property or gold becomes YOUR taxable long-term capital gain.

Illustration: Your grandfather bought a property for ₹8 lakh in 2003. You sell it today for ₹60 lakh. Capital gain ≈ ₹52 lakh. Tax at 12.5% LTCG ≈ ₹6.5 lakh — even though you paid nothing for it. (Actual tax depends on dates, the indexation option available, and your specific situation.)

Two things that can reduce the bill: (1) Indexation benefit may apply on property or gold acquired before July 23, 2024; (2) Section 54 exemption if you reinvest sale proceeds in another residential property.

What to do: Dig out the original purchase deed and have a CA calculate your liability BEFORE the sale deed is registered — restructuring after the fact is rarely possible.

Drop us a message on WhatsApp — we'll walk you through your numbers before you sign anything.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

Source: Sections 45, 49 & 55(2)(b), Income Tax Act 1961.

slides.label: MONEY MYTH
slides.image_prompt: An elderly Indian woman gently handing a set of old brass house keys to her adult daughter in a warmly lit traditional Indian home living room, faded property documents spread on a wooden table between them, soft golden afternoon light streaming through a window
slides.headline: Inherited property? Great. Now watch out for the TAX trap.

slides.label: MYTH vs FACT
slides.myth: You inherited the house or gold — you didn't earn it, you didn't buy it, so selling it must be tax-free.
slides.truth: Inheritance itself is tax-free. But the moment you sell, Long-Term Capital Gains tax applies — and the entire price rise since your ancestor first bought it counts as YOUR gain.

slides.label: TAX FILES
slides.headline: The clock started the day your ANCESTOR bought it — not the day you inherited.
slides.body: Under Section 49, the original owner's cost of purchase and date of acquisition transfer directly to you. Decades of price appreciation show up as your capital gain — even though you paid nothing.

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
slides.body: Illustrative LTCG tax if you sell a property your grandfather bought for ₹8L in 2003, now worth ₹60L. (For illustration only — actual tax depends on dates, regime chosen & indexation option.)

slides.label: REAL TALK
slides.image_prompt: Close-up of a worn, yellowed original property sale deed from the early 1990s resting on a dark wooden desk beside a modern calculator, a magnifying glass placed on the document, warm directional side lighting casting a soft shadow
slides.headline: Your ancestor's old deed is WORTH money today.
slides.caption: It proves the original purchase price — which legally reduces your taxable capital gain.
slides.body: It proves the original purchase price — which legally reduces your taxable capital gain.

slides.label: TAX PLANNING
slides.title: Before you sell inherited property or gold
points: Dig out the original purchase deed — however old
Note the year of purchase: sets holding period & indexation
Bought before 2001? Use Fair Market Value as on April 1, 2001 as cost
Reinvesting in a new house? Check Section 54 exemption
